title: How to hold a crochet hook
subtitle: Pencil grip and knife grip, plus how to tension the yarn.
excerpt: Crocheters hold the hook one of two ways, and tension the yarn through the fingers of the other hand. The grip is whichever is comfortable; the tension is what makes the stitches even.
sourceNotes:
Methods drawn from two public-domain references:

• Weldon's Practical Crochet (first series, 1880s), opening pages on holding the hook and yarn. Internet Archive.
• Caulfeild & Saward, The Dictionary of Needlework (1882), entry for Crochet, sub-entry on the position of the hands. Internet Archive.

recipeTools.notes: a 4 mm hook for practise (3.5 to 5 mm is fine).

glossaryTerms.definition: Holding the crochet hook the way you hold a pencil. The hook's flat thumb rest sits between thumb and index finger; the other fingers wrap underneath.
Holding the crochet hook the way you hold a butter knife. The hook lies along the palm, gripped overhand. Common with larger hooks and chunkier yarn.
The strand of yarn coming off the ball that feeds into each stitch. Distinct from the tail (the short cut end left after the slip knot).

content.text: Crochet is a one-handed craft on the hook side and a tensioning craft on the yarn side. The hook holds the working loop and pulls new loops through. The other hand controls the yarn so each new loop comes out the same size as the last. Both halves matter, and both have established conventions.
There is no single correct grip. The two common positions are the
pencil grip
and the
knife grip
. Both produce identical fabric. Try each for a few rounds; the one you reach for without thinking after fifteen minutes is yours.
What you need

A 4 mm crochet hook and a ball of smooth, light-coloured DK or aran yarn. Light colours make the loops easier to see while you practise. Cotton or a cotton-blend is grippier than wool and shows the stitch shape more clearly at this stage.
Pencil grip

Hold the hook between thumb and index finger as if it were a pencil. The flat thumb rest (the indented patch about 5 cm from the hooked end) sits exactly under the pad of the thumb. The middle finger sits on top of the shaft for stability. The other two fingers curl underneath.

Pencil grip suits smaller hooks (3 mm and under), finer yarns, and lacework, where the hook needs to rotate freely for chains and picots. Most British and continental European crocheters learn pencil grip first.
Knife grip

Hold the hook in a closed fist the way you hold a butter knife. The shaft lies along the palm; the thumb sits on top of the thumb rest; the four fingers wrap underneath. The wrist drives the motion rather than the fingers.
Knife grip suits larger hooks (5 mm and up), chunkier yarns, and long sessions where the wrist tires less than the fingers do. Many American crocheters learn knife grip first.
Tensioning the yarn
Whichever hand the hook is in, the other hand controls the
working yarn
. The standard tension path is:
The working yarn comes off the ball, runs over the back of the index finger, around the outside of the pinkie, and back across the palm.
The thumb and middle finger of the same hand hold the fabric at the working stitch, just below where the hook is forming the next loop.
The index finger holds the yarn at about a 30-degree angle above the work. This presents the yarn for the hook to catch on each stitch. The pinkie controls how freely the yarn feeds off the ball.

If the yarn slips through the fingers freely with no resistance, the stitches will be loose and floppy. If the yarn drags, the stitches will be tight and hard to push the hook through. The right tension is somewhere in between: even, consistent, and the same from the start of a row to the end.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong while learning the grip
attrs.intro: Four things every new crocheter does at some point.

items.symptom: Hand cramp after a few minutes
items.cause: The grip is too tight, or the hook is being squeezed instead of held.
items.fix: Loosen the grip until the hook is held just firmly enough that it doesn't drop. Cramp clears within a session of looser holding. If a specific grip keeps cramping, try the other.

items.symptom: The yarn comes off the ball in fits and starts
items.cause: Pinkie tension is too tight, causing the yarn to bunch and release.
items.fix: Loosen the pinkie wrap. The pinkie's job is to slow the yarn, not stop it. The index finger does the actual tension control.

items.symptom: The hook can't catch the yarn on each stitch
items.cause: The yarn is being held too flat, parallel to the fabric, instead of presented at an angle the hook can reach.
items.fix: Lift the index finger so the working yarn rises at about 30 degrees above the work. The hook should be able to scoop the yarn without rotating the wrist.

items.symptom: Stitch size varies across a row
items.cause: Tension changed within the row, usually because the hand readjusted mid-row.
items.fix: Pause every five stitches and check the tension feels consistent before continuing. With practice the consistency becomes automatic; in the first few sessions it needs conscious attention.

content.text: Where this goes next
Once the grip and tension feel comfortable on a slip knot and a row of chains, the first proper stitch to learn is
the treble

(UK: treble, US: double crochet). From there the rest of the basic stitch family (double crochet, half treble, double treble) follows from the same hook motions, just with different numbers of yarn-overs.
